---
title: "Part 7: Iterative Solvers and Memory Strategy"
bibliography: ../../references.bib
---

[Back to regularizeNd series home](index.qmd)

[Previous: Part 6](part-06-constraints-and-mappings.qmd)

This part focuses on the practical decision of which solver to use and why memory and factorization
can become the binding constraint for large problems.

## Practical Goal

1. Choose between direct and iterative solvers based on problem scale.
2. Understand when memory limits dominate solver selection.
3. Apply a repeatable fallback strategy when direct solves are impractical.

## Example Anchor

Primary script: `regularizeNd/Examples/exampleIterativeSolver.m`

Download the example data file [`a01_etopo_loihi.mat`](a01_etopo_loihi.mat) to run this script.

## The Full Solver Menu

`regularizeNd` accepts the `solver` argument as the sixth positional parameter:

```matlab
yGrid = regularizeNd(x, y, xGrid, smoothness, interpMethod, solver)
```

| Solver | Type | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| `"normal"` | Direct (normal equations) | **Default recommendation.** ~3× faster than `\` for well-conditioned systems; regularizeNd generates well-conditioned systems in the overwhelming majority of cases. |
| `"\"` | Direct (backslash / sparse QR-LU) | Most robust; fall back when `"normal"` produces unexpected results. |
| `"pcg"` | Iterative (conjugate gradient) | Large problems where direct solvers exhaust RAM. First iterative choice. |
| `"symmlq"` | Iterative (symmetric LQ) | Very similar to `pcg`; try if `pcg` stalls or diverges. |
| `"lsqr"` | Iterative (least-squares QR) | Last resort when `pcg` and `symmlq` fail; solves $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ directly rather than the normal equations. Slower but most numerically stable of the three iterative options. |

## Why Normal Equations Are Faster Here

The `"normal"` solver forms $A^\top A \mathbf{x} = A^\top \mathbf{b}$ and solves a symmetric
positive-definite (SPD) system via Cholesky factorization.  The regularizeNd system is
well-conditioned by design (the smoothness penalty adds a positive-definite correction), so the
squared condition number of the normal equations is rarely a problem in practice.

The `"\"` solver uses MATLAB's sparse direct solver on the full rectangular $A$ matrix — more
general but roughly 2–3× slower for the structure regularizeNd produces.

## Why Iterative Solvers Exist

For a 5-D grid with moderate resolution, the system can have 100 000+ unknowns.  Storing the full
Cholesky factor ($O(n^2)$ in the worst case) can exhaust RAM.  Iterative solvers need only
matrix-vector products, so memory scales as $O(n)$.

The tradeoff is convergence: iterative solvers need preconditioning to converge in a reasonable
number of iterations.  regularizeNd uses an incomplete Cholesky preconditioner internally.  The
preconditioner is the dominant reason `"pcg"` and `"symmlq"` are practical even at large scale.

Measured timing from the `exampleIterativeSolver.m` reference script (5-D, ~117 600 grid points):

| Solver | Approximate solve time |
|---|---|
| `"normal"` | ~930 s |
| `"\"` | ~8 266 s |
| `"pcg"` | ~53 s |
| `"symmlq"` | ~53 s |
| `"lsqr"` | ~378 s |

The iterative solvers are 12–17× faster than the direct options at that scale.

## Controlling Iterative Solver Behavior

Two additional optional positional arguments are available:

```matlab
yGrid = regularizeNd(x, y, xGrid, smoothness, interpMethod, solver, ...
    maxIterations, solverTolerance)
```

| Argument | Default | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `maxIterations` | solver default | Maximum number of iterations before early exit |
| `solverTolerance` | solver default | Relative residual tolerance for convergence |

Tighter tolerances increase iteration count and runtime.  For engineering lookup tables `1e-6` is
almost always sufficient.

## Decision Tree

```
Start: pick solver
  │
  ├─ Is the grid smaller than ~50 000 nodes?
  │     YES → use "normal"   (fast, robust for this scale)
  │     NO  → continue
  │
  ├─ Does "normal" run out of memory or produce a warning?
  │     NO  → keep "normal"
  │     YES → try "pcg"
  │               Converges?  YES → done
  │               Converges?  NO  → try "symmlq"
  │                                     Converges?  YES → done
  │                                     Converges?  NO  → try "lsqr"
  │
  └─ If all iterative options fail → reduce grid resolution or
       split the problem into smaller subdomains
```

## Compression Approach for Very Large Grids

If you cannot reduce grid size without sacrificing quality, two practical strategies:

1. **Dimension-by-dimension refinement** — keep coarse in all dimensions except the one that most
   drives quality, then refine that dimension alone.  The grid count grows linearly, not
   exponentially.

2. **Local fitting** — partition the domain into overlapping subdomains, fit each independently,
   and stitch with a smooth blending function.  Not built into regularizeNd but straightforward to
   implement around it.

## Memory Footprint Estimate

A rough estimate of peak memory before the solve step for an $n$-node grid:

| Component | Memory |
|---|---|
| $A_\text{fidelity}$ (sparse) | $\approx 2^D \cdot N_\text{data} \cdot 8$ bytes (linear interp) |
| $L_\text{reg}$ per dimension | $\approx 3 \cdot n \cdot 8$ bytes (tridiagonal) |
| $A^\top A$ (SPD, direct) | $\approx n^2 \cdot 8$ bytes worst case |

Where $D$ is the number of input dimensions and $N_\text{data}$ is the scatter data count.  The
$A^\top A$ term is the one that kills direct solvers at large $n$.

## Solver Sweep — Small-Scale Reference

The following code sweeps all five solvers on the Loihi bathymetry data with a moderately fine
grid.  Run it to confirm all solvers return the same answer and to benchmark relative timing on
your hardware.

```matlab
clc; clear; close all;

data = load('a01_etopo_loihi.mat');
x = double(data.x);
y = double(data.y);
z = double(data.z);

xGrid = {linspace(min(x), max(x), 60), linspace(min(y), max(y), 61)};
smoothness = 1e-4;
interpMethod = "linear";

solvers = ["normal", "\", "pcg", "symmlq", "lsqr"];
times   = zeros(1, numel(solvers));
grids   = cell(1, numel(solvers));

for k = 1:numel(solvers)
    s = solvers(k);
    if ismember(s, ["pcg", "symmlq", "lsqr"])
        tic
        grids{k} = regularizeNd([x,y], z, xGrid, smoothness, interpMethod, s, ...
            500, 1e-6);
        times(k) = toc;
    else
        tic
        grids{k} = regularizeNd([x,y], z, xGrid, smoothness, interpMethod, s);
        times(k) = toc;
    end
    fprintf('%-8s  %.3f s\n', s, times(k));
end

% Verify all grids agree
for k = 2:numel(solvers)
    rmseDiff = sqrt(mean((grids{k}(:) - grids{1}(:)).^2));
    fprintf('RMSE vs normal: %-8s  %.3e\n', solvers(k), rmseDiff);
end
```

Expected result: RMSE values should all be below `1e-4` relative to the reference grid, confirming
that solver choice does not affect the answer at normal problem scales.

## Checklist Before Leaving This Part

- [ ] Have you confirmed `"normal"` works for your problem size?
- [ ] If RAM is the constraint, have you tried `"pcg"` with `maxIterations` and `solverTolerance`?
- [ ] Have you verified that the iterative result matches the direct result to your required tolerance?
- [ ] Have you estimated grid node count and peak memory before scaling up?

## Next

[Part 8: Lineage and Practical Migration Notes](part-08-lineage-and-practical-migration-notes.qmd)
